name: Design Thinking / Double Diamond
year_developed: 2004 by UK Design Council (Double Diamond framework); design thinking philosophy synthesized and popularized by IDEO and Tim Brown from 1990s-2009 (Brown's 'Change by Design'); Double Diamond celebrated 20th anniversary in 2023 and released under Creative Commons licence; expanded Framework for Innovation released 2021
progenitor: UK Design Council (Double Diamond, 2004) — developed under Richard Eisermann, Director of Design and Innovation, who tasked his team to answer 'How do we describe the design process?'; IDEO (design thinking philosophy) — Tim Brown (CEO/Co-Chair of IDEO) and David Kelley (founder of IDEO and Stanford d.school) are the most influential proponents; the Double Diamond team acknowledged 'standing on the shoulders of giants,' incorporating prior design process models from academia and industry
driving_insight: The central insight is that problem-solving requires explicit alternation between divergent thinking (opening up, exploring broadly, generating options) and convergent thinking (narrowing down, selecting, refining). Most problem-solving failures come from either: (a) converging too early — locking into a solution before the problem is properly understood (the most common failure mode); or (b) diverging indefinitely — never committing to a direction. The Double Diamond enforces this alternation: DIVERGE to understand the problem space (Discover), CONVERGE to define the specific problem (Define), DIVERGE to generate solution options (Develop), CONVERGE to deliver the chosen solution (Deliver). The methodology is human-centered at every phase — real user needs, observed in context, drive both problem definition and solution evaluation. It is also fundamentally iterative — the diamonds are not a one-way pipeline; loops back to earlier phases are expected when new insights emerge. The driving insight is that design is not decoration applied at the end — it is a disciplined process for understanding and solving problems, applicable far beyond traditional design domains.
decision_style: deliberative and human-centered — decisions are driven by user research, prototyping feedback, and iterative testing, not by authority or algorithmic optimization. The divergent phases are deliberately non-judgmental — quantity and variety are valued over quality during brainstorming and exploration. The convergent phases apply structured synthesis: affinity diagramming, user journey mapping, 'How Might We' framing, and iterative prototyping with real user feedback. The process is hierarchical in that a design lead typically guides the team and makes final convergence decisions, but those decisions are evidence-based (user data, test results) rather than opinion-based. The alternation between divergent and convergent thinking is the core decision-making rhythm — it prevents both premature closure and endless exploration.
problem_class_targeted: ill-defined, human-centered problems where the nature of the problem is as uncertain as the nature of the solution; 'wicked problems' where stakeholders disagree on what the problem even is; innovation challenges where existing solutions are inadequate and novel approaches are needed; problems where user/customer experience is central to success; well-suited for product design, service design, organizational transformation, and social innovation; less suited for: well-defined technical problems with clear specifications (traditional engineering); problems where user needs are already thoroughly understood and documented; problems requiring immediate crisis response without time for research and iteration
ordered_phases: Four phases across two diamonds. Diamond 1 — THE PROBLEM SPACE ('Design the right thing'): (1) DISCOVER (divergent) — explore the problem broadly; conduct user research through interviews, observations, immersions, surveys; analyze market trends, competitive landscape, and technology opportunities; the goal is to understand real user needs and context, not to assume; methods include ethnography, diary studies, user shadowing, analytics review. (2) DEFINE (convergent) — synthesize Discover insights into a clear, actionable problem statement; filter and prioritize findings; use affinity mapping, personas, journey maps, and 'How Might We' questions; the output is a defined problem that is specific enough to solve but broad enough to allow creative solutions. Diamond 2 — THE SOLUTION SPACE ('Design the thing right'): (3) DEVELOP (divergent) — generate multiple potential solutions; brainstorm, co-design with users and stakeholders, create low-fidelity prototypes; test at small scale; iterate rapidly; quantity over quality initially; fail early and cheaply. (4) DELIVER (convergent) — refine, test at increasing fidelity, and implement the chosen solution; scale what works, deprecate what doesn't; gather feedback and iterate; launch and monitor. The process is NOT strictly linear — teams loop back as new insights emerge, and in a digital world, products are never truly 'finished.'
feedback_loops: Feedback loops are explicit and built into the methodology: (1) User testing at every phase creates immediate feedback — prototypes in Develop and Deliver are tested with users, and results feed back into iteration; (2) The diamonds themselves imply loops — you may Discover, Define, Develop a prototype, test it, and realize you defined the wrong problem, sending you back to Discover; (3) The iterative prototyping cycle (build -> test -> learn -> build) is a tight, fast feedback loop at the Develop/Deliver boundary; (4) Post-launch monitoring and user feedback create ongoing loops after Deliver; (5) The methodology emphasizes that 'no idea is ever truly finished' — products are continuously iterated based on user behavior and feedback; (6) Co-design with users embeds feedback directly into the design process rather than treating it as a separate review step; (7) The loops are explicit (testing is a named activity) and emergent (any phase can loop back to any prior phase). The feedback-rich nature of the methodology is its primary mechanism for reducing the risk of building the wrong thing.
gates: No formal approval gates — Design Thinking is a philosophy and process, not a governance framework. In practice, informal checkpoints occur at: (1) The Discover-to-Define convergence — the team must agree they have sufficient user understanding to frame the problem; (2) The Define-to-Develop transition — stakeholders must agree on the problem statement before solution development begins; (3) The Develop-to-Deliver convergence — the chosen solution must demonstrate viability through testing; (4) Launch readiness — the solution must meet quality, business, and user acceptance criteria. These checkpoints are typically collaborative and evidence-based (user testing data), not formal tollgate reviews. When a 'gate blocks' (e.g., user testing reveals the solution doesn't work): the team iterates — returning to Develop for alternative solutions or to Define if the problem statement was wrong. There is no certification body, no formal sign-off process, and criteria are project-specific. The methodology is deliberately lightweight — gates should not become bureaucratic obstacles to iteration.
exit_criteria: The Double Diamond process is 'done' when: (1) The solution has been delivered to users and meets the defined success criteria; (2) User feedback confirms the solution addresses the defined problem; (3) The business outcomes (if applicable) are being achieved; (4) The team has captured learnings for future iterations. However, in practice, digital products and services are never truly 'finished' — the process continues with new cycles of Discover-Define-Develop-Deliver as user needs evolve and new opportunities emerge. Partial completion: (a) A project may deliver an MVP (Minimum Viable Product) that addresses the core problem, with further iterations planned; (b) Research may conclude that the problem is not worth solving (the Discover phase reveals it's not a real user need), which is a valid and valuable outcome; (c) Prototyping may reveal that no viable solution exists within constraints. The methodology values learning over completion — a 'failed' project that produces valuable insights about users is more successful than a 'completed' project that built the wrong thing.
decomposition_method: Design Thinking decomposes through user-need-driven and experience-driven methods rather than task decomposition: (1) In Discover, the problem space is decomposed by user segments, user journeys, and touchpoints — what are the distinct user types, what are their goals, what is their end-to-end experience?; (2) Journey mapping decomposes the user experience into stages (awareness, consideration, purchase, use, support, etc.) — each stage is a sub-problem; (3) In Define, affinity diagramming clusters research findings into themes — these themes become the dimensions of the problem; (4) 'How Might We' questions decompose the big problem statement into actionable design challenges; (5) In Develop, the solution is decomposed into features/epics for prototyping — but this is emergent from ideation, not a prescribed hierarchical breakdown; (6) Decomposition is domain-driven (user needs), experience-driven (journey stages), and value-driven (what matters to users). It is NOT dependency-driven task decomposition — the methodology explicitly avoids breaking the problem down into technical subsystems. The decomposition method is qualitative and user-anchored rather than quantitative and system-anchored.
granularity_guidance: Granularity is governed by user impact and testability: (1) In Discover, research continues until saturation — new interviews stop yielding new insights (typically 5-15 users per segment for qualitative research); (2) In Define, the problem statement should be specific enough to be solvable but broad enough to allow creative freedom — 'How might we help elderly users manage their medications?' rather than 'Build a pill reminder app'; (3) In Develop, prototypes range from low-fidelity (paper sketches, 1 hour) to high-fidelity (interactive, days) — granularity increases as uncertainty decreases; (4) Features for delivery are broken down to the level where they can be built, tested, and iterated within a sprint (1-2 weeks); (5) There is no 8-80 hour rule equivalent — the iteration cycle (build-measure-learn) determines granularity; (6) The stopping rule for decomposition: 'Can we build and test this within one iteration cycle, and will the test results inform the next decision?' If yes, the granularity is sufficient. The methodology values learning velocity over decomposition completeness.
boundary_rules: Boundaries are set by the user scope and project brief: (1) The Discover phase is bounded by the target user group(s) and the domain of inquiry — scope is defined in the initial brief but may expand if research reveals critical adjacent needs; (2) The Define phase explicitly narrows boundaries — 'this is the specific problem we are solving for this specific user group'; (3) Business viability, technical feasibility, and user desirability are the three boundary lenses — a solution must satisfy all three; (4) Overlap between projects is managed at the portfolio level — related user needs may spawn separate design projects; (5) MECE is not enforced — different design projects may explore overlapping problem spaces, and this is acceptable because convergence happens at the solution level; (6) Boundary violations are caught through user testing — if the solution doesn't solve the defined problem for the target user, the boundaries were too broad or too narrow; (7) The methodology values flexible boundaries — insights that don't fit the current project may seed future projects. Clean boundaries are less important than user-centered outcomes.
risk_assessment_mechanism: Risk assessment is embedded in the iterative prototyping approach: (1) The biggest risk in design is building the wrong thing — the Double Diamond mitigates this by investing heavily in problem understanding (Discover/Define) before solution building; (2) Low-fidelity prototyping mitigates the risk of over-investing in wrong solutions — fail early, fail cheaply; (3) User testing at every iteration provides continuous risk feedback — desirability risk is tested directly with users; (4) The three lenses (Desirability, Feasibility, Viability) provide a risk taxonomy — each lens identifies a different class of risk; (5) Co-design and stakeholder involvement mitigate adoption risk — users and stakeholders who participate in design are more likely to adopt the solution; (6) Risk identification is qualitative and human-judgment-based — there is no formal FMEA, probabilistic scoring, or risk register; (7) The methodology handles known risks through iteration and testing; it handles unknown risks (unknown unknowns) by revealing them through user research. The primary risk mechanism is 'test with real users, early and often' — this catches risks that no amount of internal analysis would surface.
unknown_handling: When encountering unknowns: (1) In Discover, unknowns are the starting point — 'we don't know what users need' is why research is conducted; (2) Probe through research — user interviews, observation, and ethnography convert unknowns into insights; (3) In Develop, unknowns about solution viability are resolved through prototyping and testing — 'we don't know which approach works' is resolved by testing multiple approaches; (4) Default-permissive with small bets — try multiple low-cost experiments rather than committing to one unproven direction; (5) The methodology embraces ambiguity — 'design thinkers are comfortable with not knowing the answer at the start'; (6) Unknowns that cannot be resolved through available research are acknowledged and managed as assumptions — 'we assume users will behave this way; we will validate in the next test'; (7) The methodology handles unknowns by making them actionable — every unknown is a research question or a prototype to build. Unknowns are the fuel of the design process, not obstacles to it.
default_stance_on_unknown: PERMISSIVE with structured exploration — the methodology encourages action despite uncertainty because the fastest way to resolve unknowns is through prototyping and testing, not through analysis. The mantra is 'build to think' — create something, test it with users, and learn. However, the action is scoped: (1) Low-fidelity, low-cost prototypes in early stages limit exposure; (2) Small sample sizes (5-15 users) provide directional feedback without requiring statistical significance; (3) Decisions are explicitly reversible — 'this is an experiment, not a commitment'; (4) The bias is toward learning through making rather than learning through analyzing. This is a deliberate contrast with analytical methodologies like DMAIC that require measurement baselines before action — Design Thinking argues that for ill-defined problems, the baseline cannot be established without exploratory action. The permissive stance is bounded by the iteration cycle — you can act on uncertainty for one sprint, but you must test and learn before the next.
safety_guardrails: Safety mechanisms are process-based and user-centered: (1) User testing is the primary safety mechanism — any solution that harms or fails users will be revealed in testing before full deployment; (2) The three-lens framework (Desirability, Feasibility, Viability) prevents solutions that users want but can't be built or sustained; (3) Iterative, incremental development contains blast radius — a failed iteration only wastes one sprint, not the entire project; (4) Co-design and stakeholder involvement reduce the risk of organizational rejection; (5) The 'fail early, fail cheaply' philosophy explicitly accepts that individual iterations may fail, but structures the process so that failure is contained; (6) For LLM/agent contexts: no I/O firewalls, sandboxing, or technical guardrails exist — the methodology was designed for human design teams; (7) The primary guardrail is the tight feedback loop with real users — in the absence of real users (e.g., an LLM agent designing a solution autonomously), the methodology loses its primary safety mechanism.
human_decision_points: Humans are embedded at every phase — Design Thinking is fundamentally anthropocentric. Specific human decision points: (1) User research — humans (users) provide the foundational data through interviews, observations, and testing; (2) Synthesis — humans interpret research findings, identify patterns, and frame the problem (Define); (3) Ideation — humans generate creative solutions (Develop); (4) Prototype creation — humans design and build prototypes; (5) User testing — humans (users) interact with prototypes and provide feedback; (6) Convergence decisions — humans decide which solutions to pursue and which to abandon; (7) Business/technical validation — humans assess viability and feasibility. Users are IN the loop as research subjects and test participants. Designers are IN the loop as researchers, synthesizers, and creators. The methodology is entirely dependent on human creativity, empathy, and judgment. It cannot function without human users (whose needs drive the process) and human designers (who exercise creative judgment).
override_capability: Yes, at multiple levels: (1) User feedback can override any design direction — 'users don't want this' is the ultimate override; (2) The design lead can redirect the team at any convergence point; (3) Stakeholders can halt or redirect based on business viability concerns; (4) Technical leads can override based on feasibility; (5) Override granularity: per-feature (remove or change a feature based on user testing), per-solution-direction (pivot to a different solution approach), per-problem-definition (redefine the problem based on research insights), per-project (cancel if the problem proves unsolvable or not worth solving). The methodology explicitly normalizes override — iteration IS continuous override based on evidence. There is no automated override because all processes are human-executed.
fallback_when_no_human: Design Thinking fundamentally cannot function without human users and human designers. If users are unavailable: (1) The methodology loses its evidentiary foundation — design decisions without user input are speculation; (2) Personas and journey maps can partially substitute for absent users (if based on prior research), but they are pale substitutes; (3) The methodology degrades to opinion-based design — 'I think users want X' — which is what the methodology was designed to prevent. If designers are unavailable: (1) No new research, synthesis, ideation, or prototyping occurs; (2) Existing solutions may be maintained but not improved. In an LLM-agent context: an LLM can simulate the discovery process (generate user personas, hypothesize needs, suggest solutions), can generate prototypes (especially for digital products), and can simulate user testing (predict user reactions). LLM-based Design Thinking would be faster and cheaper than human-led Design Thinking, but would lack the ground-truth connection to real users that is the methodology's defining feature and primary safety mechanism.
max_problem_size: Demonstrated at national scale — UK Government Digital Service used the Double Diamond for nation-wide digital service transformation (GOV.UK); IDEO has applied design thinking to entire healthcare systems, education systems, and urban planning; the UK Design Council's Framework for Innovation explicitly addresses systemic challenges embedded in complex social, economic, and environmental systems. Practical limits: (1) A single design project typically addresses one user group's needs within one domain — scope is bounded by the ability to conduct meaningful user research; (2) Large-scale transformations are decomposed into multiple design projects coordinated at the program/portfolio level; (3) What breaks at scale: the user research bottleneck — ethnography and in-depth interviews don't scale linearly; you cannot deeply understand millions of users without segmentation and sampling; (4) The prototype-and-test cycle becomes unwieldy for physical infrastructure projects (you can't prototype a bridge at low fidelity). The theoretical limit is the boundary of meaningful user research — if you cannot observe and talk to representative users, the methodology loses its foundation.
parallel_execution: Yes, parallelism is natural and expected: (1) Different design teams can run parallel Design Thinking cycles for different user groups, products, or features; (2) Within a project, multiple prototypes can be developed and tested in parallel; (3) User research (Discover) can be parallelized across multiple researchers and user segments; (4) Dependencies between parallel streams are resolved at integration points — typically at convergence moments (Define and Deliver); (5) Parallelism is first-class in the Develop phase (multiple solutions explored simultaneously) but sequential across diamonds (you should Define before you Develop); (6) The methodology supports organizational-scale parallelism trivially because each design cycle is self-contained for a user-problem pair. However, coordinating parallel design streams requires explicit integration effort to ensure consistency across user touchpoints.
team_scaling_model: Sub-linear scaling with optimal team sizes: (1) A design team is most effective with 4-8 core members representing design, research, technology, and business perspectives; (2) Larger projects use multiple design teams working on different aspects (e.g., different user journeys); (3) The 'two-pizza rule' (teams small enough to be fed by two pizzas, ~6-10 people) is commonly applied; (4) Scaling beyond a single team requires coordination roles — design leads, design ops, and design systems to ensure consistency; (5) User research doesn't scale linearly — interviewing more users provides diminishing returns (qualitative research saturates at ~5-15 users per segment); (6) Negative scaling occurs when: (a) too many designers on one problem create conflicting directions; (b) coordination overhead exceeds productive design time; (c) the design process becomes a political negotiation rather than a user-centered exploration. The methodology scales through multiple small, autonomous teams rather than large single teams.
cost_scaling_model: Human-time-intensive: (1) User research: ethnographic studies, interviews, and observations require significant researcher time (days to weeks per project); (2) Synthesis: affinity mapping and problem framing are labor-intensive group activities; (3) Prototyping: costs vary from near-zero (paper sketches) to significant (interactive digital prototypes, physical mockups); (4) User testing: recruiting participants, conducting sessions, and analyzing results require ongoing investment; (5) Costs scale linearly with the number of design cycles and user research depth; (6) For LLM-based Design Thinking: cost would scale with the number of prompts required for research simulation, persona generation, ideation, and testing simulation; (7) The dominant cost is human creative time — this is intentional because human creativity and empathy are the methodology's core value proposition; (8) The methodology argues that investing in Discovery and Definition (understanding the problem) saves exponentially more in Development and Delivery (not building the wrong thing). The cost is front-loaded but the methodology claims total lifecycle cost reduction.
replanning_built_in: Replanning is inherent and continuous: (1) Every user test is a potential replanning trigger — if users don't respond as expected, the design is revised; (2) The iterative cycle (prototype -> test -> learn -> revise) is continuous replanning at the feature/solution level; (3) Major replanning (return to Define or Discover) occurs when testing reveals the problem was misidentified; (4) The Double Diamond framework explicitly normalizes loops back to earlier phases; (5) Replanning is both local (revise a feature) and global (redefine the problem); (6) The methodology does not distinguish between 'planning' and 'replanning' — all design decisions are provisional and subject to revision based on user evidence; (7) Replanning is a first-class, continuous activity — the methodology IS a replanning process. This is one of the Double Diamond's greatest strengths — it is designed for continuous adaptation to user needs.
trigger_mechanism: Adaptation is triggered by: (1) User feedback — the primary trigger; when testing reveals that users don't understand, don't want, or can't use the solution; (2) New research insights — as the team learns more about users, earlier assumptions are revised; (3) Prototype failure — when a prototype doesn't achieve the intended outcome; (4) Stakeholder input — business or technical constraints that require design revision; (5) Market/environment changes — new competitors, technologies, or user behaviors; (6) The trigger mechanism is human-detected and continuous — every test, every observation, every conversation may trigger adaptation. There are no automated triggers. The methodology's short iteration cycles (days to weeks) mean that triggers fire frequently and adaptation is rapid.
adaptation_cost: Low to moderate, depending on scope: (1) Feature-level adaptation (changing a UI element based on user testing): very low — hours of redesign; (2) Solution-level adaptation (pivoting to a different solution approach): moderate — days to weeks of rework, but previous research insights remain valid; (3) Problem-level adaptation (redefining the problem): high — returns the team to Define/Discover, but the existing user research is still valuable; (4) State preservation is good — research insights, personas, and journey maps are assets that survive solution pivots; (5) Lost work is contained at the feature/solution level — a prototype you abandon taught you something, so it wasn't wasted; (6) The methodology explicitly budgets for adaptation — iteration is expected, and timelines include buffer for multiple cycles. The low adaptation cost is by design — the prototype-and-test cycle is engineered for rapid, cheap revision. The primary cost of replanning is the time spent on the cycle, which is already factored into the methodology.
failure_recovery_mechanism: Recovery is an extension of the normal iterative cycle: (1) Detection — failure is detected through user testing (doesn't solve the problem) or metrics (doesn't achieve outcomes); (2) Isolation — trace the failure to its source: was the solution wrong (Develop/Deliver failure), the problem definition wrong (Define failure), or the user understanding wrong (Discover failure)?; (3) Recovery — return to the appropriate phase and re-execute: if the solution failed, Develop new solutions; if the problem was misidentified, return to Define; if users were misunderstood, return to Discover; (4) Blast radius containment — the iterative cycle (small bets, frequent testing) limits the cost of any single failure to one sprint; (5) Graceful degradation — if a new solution fails, the previous solution (or status quo) remains in place; (6) The methodology normalizes failure as learning — 'fail early, fail cheaply' implies that individual failures are expected and budgeted for; (7) There is no formal retry mechanism — iteration IS retry. Recovery is indistinguishable from normal process — you test, you learn, you revise. This tight integration of failure recovery into the normal workflow is a key strength.
output_artifacts: Varied and phase-dependent: Discover: user research reports, personas, empathy maps, user journey maps (current state), stakeholder maps, research insight decks; Define: problem statements, 'How Might We' questions, affinity diagrams, value proposition canvas, design principles; Develop: sketches, wireframes, low-fidelity prototypes, high-fidelity prototypes, co-design workshop outputs, concept testing reports; Deliver: final designs (UI specs, service blueprints, architectural plans), usability testing reports, design systems, launch plans, measurement frameworks. The Double Diamond itself is often used as a process visualization artifact — showing stakeholders where the project is in the journey. No single 'plan document' is produced — the project brief, research insights, and prototype iterations collectively form the record. Artifacts are visual and narrative-heavy — the methodology favors visual communication over text-heavy documents.
briefing_format: Visual and narrative — the standard format is the design presentation/critique. A typical briefing: (1) Frame the problem — the user need (persona, journey, pain points); (2) Show the evidence — user research clips, quotes, data; (3) Present the 'How Might We' question; (4) Show multiple solution concepts (Develop) — 'here are 3-5 ways we explored'; (5) Present the chosen direction with testing evidence — 'users responded best to Concept B, here's the usability test data'; (6) Walk through the solution — often as a prototype demonstration or user journey walkthrough; (7) Next steps — what will be tested next, what questions remain. The briefing is a conversation — stakeholders are expected to ask questions, challenge assumptions, and suggest alternatives. For executors, design specs and prototypes communicate the plan more effectively than text documents. The format is deliberately lightweight — the prototype IS the specification in mature design practices. The briefing is tailored to the audience: executives get the strategic narrative; developers get detailed specs and interactive prototypes.
traceability: Weak to moderate — depends on documentation discipline: (1) Design decisions are traceable to user research findings — 'we chose this interaction pattern because usability testing showed users struggled with Pattern A'; (2) However, the rationale is often preserved in team memory and presentation decks rather than in a formal decision record; (3) Prototypes and their testing results provide traceability — you can see what was tested, when, and what was learned; (4) Design iteration history is often preserved in version control (Figma, Sketch) or design documentation; (5) There is no formal audit trail or decision log — the methodology prioritizes speed and iteration over documentation; (6) This weak traceability is a known trade-off — stronger traceability would slow the iteration cycle, which would reduce the number of cycles and increase the risk of building the wrong thing; (7) For high-stakes projects (medical devices, aviation), Design Thinking is supplemented with formal traceability documentation (design history files, regulatory submissions).
agent_communication_protocol: Not applicable — Design Thinking is a human-centered methodology. In an LLM-agent adaptation: (1) Design artifacts (personas, journey maps, prototypes) could be serialized as structured data for inter-agent communication; (2) The divergent-convergent alternation maps to generate-then-evaluate agent patterns; (3) MCP, A2A, or custom protocols could carry design artifacts; (4) Agent discovery would need shared user model representations — consistent personas and journey stages across agents; (5) Sync communication for design critiques and convergence decisions; async for research and prototype generation; (6) Custom protocol design required — no existing standard maps to Design Thinking's artifact types. The visual nature of many design artifacts (wireframes, prototypes) creates additional serialization challenges for text-based agent communication.
learning_memory_architecture: Weak formal architecture but strong informal practice: (1) Semantic memory — design principles, patterns, and heuristics form a reusable knowledge base; design systems capture UI/UX patterns; (2) Episodic memory — each project's research insights, design decisions, and testing results are an episodic record, typically archived in shared drives or research repositories; (3) Procedural memory — design skill is procedural knowledge developed through practice and mentoring; (4) Post-hoc distillation — retrospectives and post-launch reviews extract lessons for future projects, but this is ad-hoc, not systematic; (5) The methodology has no built-in mechanism to store, index, and retrieve design knowledge across projects; (6) Design systems are the most formalized memory structure — they capture reusable components, patterns, and guidelines; (7) For LLM adaptation: a corpus of design projects (research reports, prototypes, testing results) could serve as training data; design patterns and principles are well-documented for few-shot prompting. The methodology's learning potential is high but under-exploited — most organizations lose design knowledge when designers leave. This is a significant opportunity for JORDAN pipeline enhancement.
latency_bounds: Batch to interactive — the methodology operates at different tempos: (1) Discover (user research): batch — days to weeks of fieldwork and synthesis; (2) Define (problem framing): batch — days of workshops and synthesis; (3) Develop (prototyping): interactive — rapid cycles of build (hours) -> test (hours) -> learn (hours); (4) Deliver (implementation): batch — weeks to months depending on solution scope; (5) Under time pressure: the methodology adapts by using 'Lean UX' and 'design sprints' — compressed 1-5 day cycles that approximate the Double Diamond; (6) A Google Ventures Design Sprint compresses the Double Diamond into 5 days, demonstrating that the methodology can be accelerated significantly while retaining its core structure; (7) Minimum useful cycle: a 1-day design sprint can produce useful problem definition and solution concepts; (8) The methodology cannot be used for real-time decisions but is designed for faster feedback than traditional waterfall processes. Degradation under time pressure: shortened research phases risk building solutions based on assumptions rather than evidence.
evaluation_benchmarking: No formal benchmarks — evaluation is user-centered and business-outcome-driven: (1) Usability testing metrics: task completion rate, time on task, error rate, user satisfaction (SUS, NPS); (2) Product metrics: user adoption, retention, engagement, conversion; (3) Business metrics: revenue, cost savings, customer satisfaction, market share; (4) Process metrics: number of iterations, time from concept to launch, research-to-development ratio; (5) The Design Council's 20-year track record (2004-2024) and global adoption by IDEO, government agencies, and corporations serve as organizational validation; (6) No SWE-bench, GAIA, or academic benchmark equivalents exist. For LLM-based Design Thinking: evaluation would require user testing of LLM-generated designs — can an LLM-designed solution pass usability testing with real users? This is an empirically testable question but no benchmarks currently exist.
mapping_strength: Moderate mapping to LLM agent workflows. What transfers directly: (1) The divergent-convergent alternation maps to LLM generate-then-evaluate patterns — generate many options (divergent), then evaluate and select (convergent); (2) Persona and journey map generation maps to LLM creative synthesis; (3) 'How Might We' question generation maps to LLM reframing capability; (4) Ideation and brainstorming map to LLM divergent generation; (5) Prototype description (especially for digital UIs) maps to LLM text-to-design generation; (6) The methodology's emphasis on understanding before solving maps to agent research-before-planning. What needs translation: (1) User empathy and observational research cannot be replicated by LLMs without access to real user data; (2) The physical prototyping and in-person user testing cycles cannot be automated; (3) Visual design decisions require multimodal capabilities that text-only LLMs lack; (4) The co-design and stakeholder alignment aspects require human interaction; (5) The methodology's safety mechanism (real user validation) is lost in an LLM-only implementation. Overall: the process structure maps well; the human-centered evidence base does not transfer without real user data access.
gaps: What is missing for software/LLM agent contexts: (1) No mechanism for automated user research — LLMs can simulate users but cannot ground design decisions in real user behavior; (2) No computational validation — the methodology relies on real user testing for validation; without it, design decisions are untested hypotheses; (3) The visual/prototyping dimension requires multimodal capabilities beyond text-only LLMs; (4) No formal specification output — design artifacts (wireframes, prototypes) must be translated into technical specifications for implementation; (5) The methodology assumes a creative human team — LLM creativity is derivative and may not produce novel design breakthroughs; (6) No mechanism for handling the physical world — service design includes physical touchpoints that LLMs cannot prototype or test; (7) The methodology's tolerance for ambiguity conflicts with typical software execution requirements — 'we'll figure it out through iteration' is hard to operationalize in pipelines; (8) The stakeholder alignment and political negotiation aspects of design have no computational equivalent.
adaptation_required: To apply Design Thinking to the JORDAN pipeline: (1) Incorporate explicit divergent-convergent alternation into the PLAN phase — first DIVERGE (generate multiple solution approaches), then CONVERGE (select the best based on criteria); this prevents single-solution fixation; (2) Add a 'How Might We' reframing step after RESEARCH — the agent should reframe the problem from different user/stakeholder perspectives before solving; (3) Require user-need grounding — every proposed solution must explicitly connect to a specific user need, and the agent must state what evidence supports that need; (4) Add iterative prototyping — before committing to a full solution, the agent should propose low-cost 'prototype' experiments to test critical assumptions; (5) Use the three-lens check (Desirability, Feasibility, Viability) in RISK_ASSESSMENT — is the solution desirable to users, technically feasible, and viable within constraints?; (6) The key design recommendation: Design Thinking should inform JORDAN's problem-framing and solution-exploration phases, ensuring that the agent (a) understands the problem from the user's perspective before solving, (b) generates multiple alternatives before committing, and (c) tests assumptions before full execution. The Double Diamond structure is an excellent template for the RESEARCH->DEFINE->IDEATE->EXECUTE pipeline flow.
validation_context: Industry: IDEO (global design consultancy, hundreds of projects across sectors), Apple (design-driven product development), Google (design sprints), IBM (design thinking at scale, enterprise software), Airbnb (design-driven growth). Government: UK Government Digital Service (GOV.UK transformation), US Digital Service, Danish Design Centre. Healthcare: Mayo Clinic Center for Innovation, Kaiser Permanente. Education: Stanford d.school, Harvard GSD, design programs worldwide. The Double Diamond specifically: celebrated 20 years of use (2004-2024), released under Creative Commons, adopted by NHS Digital, Meta Reality Labs Research, Braun (P&G), and thousands of organizations. Validation is primarily through practitioner adoption and case studies. The methodology has not been validated through controlled experiments comparing Design Thinking projects to non-Design-Thinking projects with matched controls.
known_failures: Documented failure modes: (1) Design theater — the organization adopts the vocabulary and tools (post-its, whiteboards, personas) without the substance (real user research, genuine iteration); it looks like Design Thinking but produces opinion-based design; (2) Research tourism — the team conducts user research but doesn't act on the findings; the research becomes an end rather than a means; (3) Solution jumping — the team rushes through Discover and Define to get to the 'fun' Develop phase; they're building solutions to poorly understood problems; (4) Endless divergence — the team keeps exploring and never converges; 'we need more research' becomes an excuse for not deciding; (5) False consensus — the Define phase produces a problem statement everyone agrees with because it's vague enough to accommodate all views; it provides no real guidance; (6) Prototype fixation — the team falls in love with an early prototype and ignores testing feedback that says it doesn't work; (7) Business/technical neglect — the team focuses exclusively on user desirability and ignores feasibility and viability; the solution is beautiful but cannot be built or sustained; (8) Stakeholder bypass — the team designs in a bubble and fails to bring stakeholders along; the solution is rejected by the organization despite solving a real user need; (9) User-as-validation (not exploration) — the team uses user testing to confirm their pre-existing ideas, not to discover new insights; confirmation bias in design; (10) Cultural mismatch — Design Thinking is introduced into organizations with command-and-control cultures that punish the experimentation and failure the methodology requires. Known counterexample: Design Thinking is less effective for problems where user needs are already well-understood and the challenge is purely technical execution — applying a human-centered discovery process to a known problem wastes time and resources.
where_still_used: Technology: Google (Design Sprints), Apple, IBM (enterprise design thinking at scale), Microsoft, Meta, Airbnb, Uber; Consulting: IDEO, Frog, Fjord (Accenture), McKinsey Design, BCG Platinion; Government: UK GDS, US Digital Service, 18F, governments of Australia, Canada, Denmark, Singapore; Healthcare: Mayo Clinic, Kaiser Permanente, NHS Digital; Education: Stanford d.school, Harvard Graduate School of Design, Royal College of Art, design programs globally; Finance: Capital One, JP Morgan Chase, BBVA; Automotive: design teams at major manufacturers. The methodology is experiencing continued growth as organizations recognize that digital transformation requires user-centered design capability, not just technology delivery. The 2021 Framework for Innovation update signals evolution toward systemic design thinking — addressing interconnected social, economic, and environmental challenges.
